items.q: What happens at a constructive (divergent) plate boundary?
items.correct: Plates move apart, magma rises to fill the gap and solidifies, forming new oceanic crust — for example at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
items.misconception: At constructive boundaries the plates collide and build mountains.
items.random_wrong: At constructive boundaries the plates slide past each other sideways.
items.why: Students confuse 'constructive' with 'collision'. Constructive means new crust is constructed; the plates move apart, not together. Sliding sideways is a conservative/transform boundary.

items.q: What is the difference between biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem?
items.correct: Biotic components are all living organisms (plants, animals, microbes); abiotic components are non-living factors (climate, soil, water, light, rock).
items.misconception: Biotic means animals and abiotic means plants.
items.random_wrong: Biotic and abiotic both refer to types of soil.
items.why: Plants are biotic, not abiotic. Students sort by familiarity rather than living vs non-living. The distinction matters because abiotic factors set the conditions for which biotic communities develop.

items.q: What is the difference between the focus and epicentre of an earthquake?
items.correct: The focus (hypocentre) is the underground point where the earthquake originates; the epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus.
items.misconception: The focus and epicentre are the same point.
items.random_wrong: The focus is at the centre of the Earth and the epicentre is on the surface.
items.why: Students treat the two terms as synonymous or place the focus deep at the Earth's core. The focus is just below the epicentre, typically only a few to tens of kilometres deep.
It's Richter. “Mercalli” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The San Andreas Fault is what type of margin?
items.why: It's Conservative. “Constructive” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What was the magnitude of the 2010 Haiti earthquake?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 Richter. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
